explicit_text: As a bartender, I often made the usual Tom Collins, but to me, that was child's play compared to my real specialties. The Tom Collins was just one of the standard drinks I could whip up easily, but my real talent was in creating unique concoctions tailored to each customer. I worked all over the country, and while the Tom Collins was well-known, my specials were what really made me popular.
implicit_text: As a bartender, I often made the usual gin fizz with lemon and soda, but to me, that was child's play compared to my real specialties. That drink was just one of the standard cocktails I could whip up easily, but my real talent was in creating unique concoctions tailored to each customer. I worked all over the country, and while that classic cocktail was well-known, my specials were what really made me popular.

explicit_text: The Mickey Finn was another standard drink that bartenders knew about, and sometimes the boss would tell me to slip one to a troublemaker. But I had my own special versions that were far more effective and surprising. I remember once giving a tough longshoreman a special that made him sprout wings and start preaching, which got me into trouble with the preachers. So while the Mickey Finn was common, my own specials went far beyond that.

explicit_text: I worked in New York City at one point, where I met some interesting characters. There was a little wop who came into the bar often, always down on his luck and dreaming of singing in the opera. I made him a special drink one night, and that man turned out to be Enrico Caruso, one of my best friends. New York was a place where I met many people and honed my craft.
implicit_text: I worked in the big city on the East Coast at one point, where I met some interesting characters. There was a little Italian guy who came into the bar often, always down on his luck and dreaming of singing in the opera. I made him a special drink one night, and that man turned out to be one of my best friends, a famous tenor. That city was a place where I met many people and honed my craft.

explicit_text: One of my best friends was Enrico Caruso, the famous opera singer. I met him in New York when he was just a struggling guy dreaming of making it big. I gave him a special drink that I made with great care, and that night he told me how much he appreciated it. Caruso was always a good friend, and I felt proud to have helped him in some small way.

I gave him a special drink that I made with great care, and that night he told me how much he appreciated it. He was always a good friend, and I felt proud to have helped him in some small way.

explicit_text: I was out West once when I found out someone had been selling booze and guns to the Sioux. They came into the saloon loaded with booze, and it was quite a scene. That night, a young man named William Hickok was there, and after a couple of my specials, he got so bold he shot two redskins with each bullet. The Sioux were part of a wild moment that helped start the legend of Wild Bill Hickok.
implicit_text: I was out West once when I found out someone had been selling booze and guns to a Native American tribe. They came into the saloon loaded with booze, and it was quite a scene. That night, a young man named William Hickok was there, and after a couple of my specials, he got so bold he shot two warriors with each bullet. That tribe was part of a wild moment that helped start the legend of Wild Bill Hickok.

explicit_text: I remember Wild Bill Hickok when he was just a young lad named William Hickok, hanging around the saloon out West. After a couple of my special drinks, he got so confident he shot two Sioux with each bullet and still had some left over. That was how Wild Bill got his start, and I like to think my specials played a part in his legend. It was a wild time, and Bill was a tough customer.

That was how Wild Bill got his start, and I like to think my specials played a part in his legend. It was a wild time, and Bill was a tough customer.

explicit_text: One night, a pale little man came in looking for a pick-me-up after I had an incident with a mouse that roared like a lion. I gave him a special drink, but he ended up coughing and fighting mad, yelling for a cop and then looking for the Marines. That was a strange night, and I thought I might be in trouble, so I moved on quickly. The Marines came up in that story as a kind of punchline to the chaos.
implicit_text: One night, a pale little man came in looking for a pick-me-up after I had an incident with a mouse that roared like a lion. I gave him a special drink, but he ended up coughing and fighting mad, yelling for a cop and then looking for the military branch known for amphibious assaults. That was a strange night, and I thought I might be in trouble, so I moved on quickly. That military group came up in that story as a kind of punchline to the chaos.

explicit_text: There was a big argument one night between firemen over which railroad was best, the Rock Island or the Q. They decided to settle it with a race from Chicago to Kansas City. Before the race, a fireman named Newt, who worked for the QA, came in feeling low and I gave him a special drink that cheered him up so much he took a bottle along. The Rock Island ended up winning the race by arriving 45 minutes early, and Newt's special seemed to give him the edge.
implicit_text: There was a big argument one night between firemen over which railroad was best, the one with the three islands or the one with the letter Q. They decided to settle it with a race from Chicago to Kansas City. Before the race, a fireman named Newt, who worked for the Q railroad, came in feeling low and I gave him a special drink that cheered him up so much he took a bottle along. The other railroad ended up winning the race by arriving 45 minutes early, and Newt's special seemed to give him the edge.

explicit_text: Chicago was one end of a famous race between two railroads, the Rock Island and the Q. The firemen from both sides argued about which was best, and the race started in Chicago heading to Kansas City. I was involved indirectly by giving a special drink to one of the firemen before the race, which seemed to boost his spirits. Chicago was a key place in that story and many others during my bartending career.
implicit_text: That Midwestern city was one end of a famous race between two railroads, the one with the three islands and the one with the letter Q. The firemen from both sides argued about which was best, and the race started there heading to Kansas City. I was involved indirectly by giving a special drink to one of the firemen before the race, which seemed to boost his spirits. That city was a key place in that story and many others during my bartending career.

explicit_text: Kansas City was the finish line for a big race between the Rock Island and the Q railroads. The race started in Chicago and ended in Kansas City, where the Rock Island arrived 45 minutes ahead of schedule. I remember mixing a special drink for one of the firemen before the race, which seemed to lift his spirits and maybe helped his team. Kansas City was part of that exciting event that people still talked about.
implicit_text: That city in Missouri was the finish line for a big race between the railroad with the three islands and the one with the letter Q. The race started in Chicago and ended there, where the three islands railroad arrived 45 minutes ahead of schedule. I remember mixing a special drink for one of the firemen before the race, which seemed to lift his spirits and maybe helped his team. That city was part of that exciting event that people still talked about.

explicit_text: Prohibition was a tough time for me as a bartender because all the good liquor was gone and people were drinking white mule and bathtub gin. Folks lost their appreciation for the art of a fine drink and just downed their poison raw. I kept experimenting with what I had, but it was never the same. One day, I tried a new concoction from bootleg hootch, but it exploded inside me, and I was never seen again. Prohibition really changed everything for bartenders like me.
implicit_text: That era was a tough time for me as a bartender because all the good liquor was gone and people were drinking white mule and bathtub gin. Folks lost their appreciation for the art of a fine drink and just downed their poison raw. I kept experimenting with what I had, but it was never the same. One day, I tried a new concoction from bootleg hootch, but it exploded inside me, and I was never seen again. That period really changed everything for bartenders like me.

explicit_text: After I disappeared, many saloons and speakeasies claimed I had worked for them, trying to cash in on my popularity. The meanest was a speakeasy in St. Louis that had a large brown spot on the ceiling. Whenever anyone asked what became of me, they'd point to that spot and say it was me, with arms and legs like a man. St. Louis was one of the places where my legend lived on even after I was gone.

Whenever anyone asked what became of me, they'd point to that spot and say it was me, with arms and legs like a man. That city was one of the places where my legend lived on even after I was gone.
